emails.body:
Team,

Attached is the v18 draft of the Validation Report. The aggregate numbers are trending in the right direction — overall error rate is holding just under 1.2% across the full test set, with rural and maritime segments comfortably below 1%. Urban remains the outlier at ~1.8%, but the gap has narrowed since v16.

I've flagged the urban subsystem confidence intervals in Section 4.3. Would appreciate your read on the narrative framing there before we lock this for the Senate briefing prep.

Josh

Josh — thanks for the quick turn. I've skimmed the executive summary and the urban caveats look appropriately bounded. One note: the projected-performance language in Section 2.1 still reads as forward-looking rather than validated. For the briefing deck we'll want to keep that distinction sharp.

I'll have consolidated feedback by COB tomorrow.

Rebecca

Josh,

Pulled the updated aggregate error rate from the v18 run — 1.15% overall, 0.87% rural/maritime, 1.82% urban. PDF attached with the revised tables and confidence bands.

Briefing deck is being built from v17 baseline per COL Abreu's guidance. Validation track looks solid for the Senate timeline. Let me know if you need the delta breakdown vs v16.

//JM
LCDR James Maddox
JIAITF

Josh,

Senate staff confirmed the briefing slot for Monday 0900. Need your final pass on v18 by Sunday evening so we can lock talking points. Rebecca — please ensure the deck aligns with the validated numbers, not the projected ones.

Current deck runs off v13 baseline; I'll forward the latest slides separately. Key message remains: ATaS delivers order-of-magnitude improvement over legacy, with aggregate error approaching 1%.

Jesse

Rebecca,

Final weekend note on v18. The attached PDF reflects the last validation cycle — aggregate 1.15%, urban 1.82% with 95% CI [1.61, 2.04]. Rural/maritime sub-1% is solid.

I'm not comfortable representing 1% as a final validated figure for the urban segment. The data supports "approaching 1% overall" with explicit urban caveats. If asked directly on Monday, I'll present the numbers as they stand.

Recommend we keep the briefing language precise: validated performance where we have it, projected where we don't.

Josh

---
Dr. Josh Brunner
Aegis Technologies
